timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — John Exiled to Patmos

sections.header: Verses 1-12 — "The Fifth Trumpet: Locusts from the Abyss"

heb.paragraph: The 'Abyss' (abyssos, vv. 1-2, 11) is the subterranean prison of demonic beings (cf. Luke 8:31, where the demons beg Jesus not to send them into the Abyss). It is distinct from Hades (the realm of the dead) and from the lake of fire (the place of final judgment, 20:10, 14-15). The Abyss functions in Revelation as the staging ground for demonic attacks on humanity: the locusts emerge from it (9:1-2), the beast ascends from it (11:7; 17:8), and Satan is imprisoned in it during the millennium (20:1-3). The key to the Abyss is given to the fallen star (v. 1)—even demonic forces can act only with divine permission, a critical theological point: Satan and his forces operate on a divine leash.

The locusts' king is named in both Hebrew (Abaddon, 'Destruction') and Greek (Apollyon, 'Destroyer')—the only bilingual naming in Revelation. Abaddon in the OT refers to the realm of the dead or destruction personified (Job 26:6; 28:22; 31:12; Ps 88:11; Prov 15:11; 27:20). The Greek Apollyon may carry a deliberate pun on Apollo (Apollōn), the Greek god associated with plagues and whose symbol was the locust—a possible dig at the imperial cult, since Domitian claimed a special relationship with Apollo. The dual naming underscores the universality of the demonic threat: destruction operates in both Jewish and Greco-Roman spheres.

refs.note: Joel's locust invasion, described as an invading army with horses, chariots, and organized ranks, provides the primary literary template. Joel's locusts are natural insects described in military language; Revelation's locusts are demonic beings described in both military and insectoid language. The reversal is significant: what Joel presented as a metaphor, Revelation literalizes as a supernatural reality.

The eighth plague of Egypt—locusts covering the land so that nothing green remained—provides the Exodus typology for the fifth trumpet. But Revelation's locusts are explicitly forbidden to damage vegetation (v. 4); their target is unrepentant humanity, not crops. The plague is worse than Egypt's: it attacks not livelihoods but souls.

Abaddon in the wisdom literature is a synonym for Sheol and destruction—the realm of death and dissolution. In Revelation 9, Abaddon is personified as the king of the demonic locusts, the angel of the Abyss. The wisdom tradition's impersonal force of destruction becomes a personal agent of demonic torment.

notes.note: The 'star that had fallen from the sky to the earth' is a fallen angel, likely Satan himself (cf. Isa 14:12; Luke 10:18). He is 'given' the key to the Abyss—even Satan operates only by divine permission. The smoke from the Abyss darkening the sun continues the de-creation theme: each trumpet undoes another element of God's good creation.

These are not natural locusts but demonic beings with supernatural power to torment. Their restrictions are significant: they cannot harm vegetation (their natural prey), they can torment only the unsealed, and they cannot kill—only inflict five months of agony. God sets strict limits on demonic activity. The torment is so severe that people seek death but cannot find it—a horrifying picture of judgment that prevents even the escape of death.

The detailed description—crowns, human faces, women's hair, lion's teeth, iron breastplates, roaring wings, scorpion tails—creates a terrifying composite image of organized, intelligent, invulnerable demonic warfare. Their king, Abaddon/Apollyon ('Destroyer'), is an angel of the Abyss. This is not chaotic evil but marshaled, hierarchical demonic power operating under a commander—yet still under God's sovereign permission.

The declaration 'the first woe is past; two other woes are yet to come' marks the structural division: the worst is still ahead. Each woe escalates in severity, demonstrating that unrepented sin leads to increasingly severe consequences.

The restrictions placed on the locusts—no vegetation, no killing, only the unsealed, only five months—teach us that evil is always bounded by divine sovereignty. Satan and his forces are not autonomous agents; they operate within parameters set by God. Even in the worst episodes of demonic oppression, God remains in control, protecting His sealed people and limiting the duration of torment. This is the comfort of divine sovereignty in the face of demonic evil.

The composite, nightmarish description of the locusts reminds us that evil, when given its head, is grotesque, organized, and relentless. The world that rejects God's protection does not find freedom but enslavement to forces far worse than the restraints it sought to escape. The choice is never between divine authority and autonomy; it is between the benevolent sovereignty of God and the malevolent tyranny of the Destroyer.

The 'star that had fallen' (astera peptokōta) uses the perfect participle, indicating a completed fall prior to the fifth trumpet. Whether this is Satan or a lesser demonic figure is debated; the parallel with Isa 14:12 (the fall of the 'morning star') and Luke 10:18 ('I saw Satan fall like lightning from heaven') supports the Satanic identification.

The explicit exemption of 'those people who did not have the seal of God on their foreheads' connects directly to the sealing of 7:1-8. The seal of God functions as spiritual protection from demonic torment—not necessarily from physical suffering (the martyrs still die, 6:9-11) but from the spiritual anguish that the locusts inflict on the unbelieving world.

Beale argues that the locust plague represents the spiritual torment that comes upon those who reject God—the internal anguish, meaninglessness, and despair of life without God. The five-month limitation and the prohibition against killing indicate that this is a temporal, penultimate judgment designed to provoke repentance (cf. 9:20-21). The Joel background is crucial: Joel's locusts were a warning judgment calling Israel to repentance, and the trumpets function similarly on a universal scale. The OT prophetic pattern of judgment-as-warning pervades the trumpet sequence. The dual naming of the king (Hebrew Abaddon, Greek Apollyon) ensures that both Jewish and Gentile readers recognize the demonic threat—no cultural exemption from the Destroyer's reach.

Osborne stresses the paradoxical nature of the demonic locusts: they are terrifyingly powerful yet strictly limited by divine decree. They cannot touch God's sealed people, they cannot kill, and their torment has a fixed duration. This paradox of unleashed-yet-leashed demonic power is central to Revelation's theology of evil: the forces of darkness are real, dangerous, and active, but they operate within boundaries set by the sovereign God. The pastoral message for the churches is clear: whatever demonic opposition you face, God has set its limits. It cannot ultimately harm those who belong to Christ.

hist.context: The fifth trumpet (first woe) releases a plague of demonic locusts from the Abyss that torment—but do not kill—those who do not have God's seal on their foreheads (v. 4). The imagery fuses three OT sources: (1) Joel 1-2's devastating locust invasion, described in military terms; (2) the eighth plague of Egypt (Exod 10:12-15), where locusts covered the land; and (3) the scorpion-like torment of supernatural beings. These are not natural insects but demonic entities: they emerge from the underworld, they have a king (natural locusts do not, Prov 30:27), they are forbidden to harm vegetation (the opposite of real locusts), and their torment is psychological ('five months' of anguish so severe that people long for death). The five-month period may correspond to the typical lifespan of a locust (May-September) or may simply indicate a limited, bounded period of torment. The key theological point is explicit: they 'were told not to harm' the sealed (v. 4)—even demonic forces obey divine parameters.

sections.header: Verses 13-21 — "The Sixth Trumpet: The Army of Two Hundred Million"

heb.gloss: twice ten thousand times ten thousand (two hundred million)
heb.paragraph: The army of 'twice ten thousand times ten thousand' (dismyriades myriadōn, v. 16) yields a number of two hundred million. Whether this is a literal count of a military force or a symbolic number indicating an innumerable host is debated. No army in the ancient world approached such a figure; even modern superpowers could not easily field such numbers. The number likely signifies an overwhelming, irresistible force—a demonic cavalry (given the description of horses with lion heads, fire-breathing mouths, and serpent tails) released at the Euphrates, the boundary river of both the Promised Land and the Roman Empire's eastern frontier. The Euphrates represents the boundary between civilization and chaos, between God's territory and enemy territory.

refs.note: God's covenant with Abraham promised the land 'from the Wadi of Egypt to the great river, the Euphrates.' The Euphrates thus marks the boundary of covenant territory. The release of destructive forces from beyond the Euphrates represents the breach of that boundary—chaos invading the ordered world, a reversal of God's covenant provision.

Isaiah used the Euphrates as a symbol of Assyrian invasion: 'the Lord is about to bring against them the mighty floodwaters of the Euphrates—the king of Assyria.' The Euphrates in prophetic literature consistently represents the source of invasion from the east—a threat that Revelation escalates to cosmic proportions.

The catalogue of sins in vv. 20-21 (idolatry, murder, sorcery, sexual immorality, theft) parallels Paul's description of humanity under God's wrath in Romans 1. Both passages describe the hardened human heart that responds to divine judgment not with repentance but with deeper entrenchment in sin.

notes.note: The voice from the four horns of the golden altar (the same altar where the saints' prayers were offered, 8:3-5) commands the release of four angels bound at the Euphrates. These are fallen angels—holy angels are not bound. They have been held in reserve for this precise moment: 'the very hour and day and month and year' (v. 15). God's timetable for judgment is precise, not approximate.

The two-hundred-million-strong cavalry combines human and demonic elements. The horses breathe fire, smoke, and sulfur—the elements of divine judgment (cf. Gen 19:24; Rev 19:20; 20:10). Their tails are like serpents, inflicting harm—an echo of the original serpent of Genesis 3. One-third of humanity is killed—an unprecedented death toll that dwarfs the fourth seal's one-fourth (6:8). The escalation is deliberate: each judgment cycle intensifies, demonstrating that God's warnings are becoming more urgent.

The most tragic verses in the chapter: despite the death of one-third of humanity, the survivors do not repent. They continue in idolatry (worshiping demons and man-made idols), murder, sorcery (pharmakeia—potentially including drug-related practices), sexual immorality, and theft. The human heart, apart from divine grace, is so hardened that even the most extreme judgment produces only greater obstinacy. This is the mystery of iniquity: sin blinds its practitioners to the very judgments designed to awaken them.

The release of the four bound angels at the Euphrates reminds us that God restrains evil until His appointed time. The forces of destruction do not operate on their own schedule but on God's. Even the most devastating judgments occur 'at the very hour and day and month and year' that God has determined. Nothing in history—not even its darkest chapters—escapes divine appointment.

The refusal to repent despite devastating judgment reveals the utter depravity of the unregenerate heart. This is the doctrine of total depravity in its starkest expression: the natural human heart is not merely indifferent to God but actively hostile, so resistant to divine overtures that even the death of one-third of humanity produces no change. Only sovereign grace can break through such hardness; judgment alone cannot accomplish repentance.

The 'four angels who are bound at the great river Euphrates' have no exact parallel in Jewish angelology. The binding suggests they are fallen angels (cf. Jude 6; 2 Pet 2:4), restrained by God until the appointed time. The Euphrates as their location connects to the river's role as the boundary of the Promised Land (Gen 15:18) and the eastern frontier of Rome—the border beyond which lay the Parthian threat.

The sin list of vv. 20-21 echoes the Decalogue's prohibitions: idolatry (commandments 1-2), murder (commandment 6), sorcery (associated with forbidden practices, Deut 18:10-12), sexual immorality (commandment 7), and theft (commandment 8). The human race violates God's fundamental moral law and refuses to acknowledge the connection between sin and judgment.

Beale notes that the sixth trumpet intensifies the Exodus typology: as Pharaoh's heart was hardened through successive plagues, so humanity's heart is hardened through the trumpet judgments. The failure to repent (vv. 20-21) is not incidental but thematic—it demonstrates that judgment without grace cannot produce transformation. The trumpets are warnings, not redemptive in themselves. Only the Lamb's sacrifice (ch. 5) and the proclamation of the gospel (chs. 10-11) can bring about genuine repentance. The sin catalogue (idolatry, murder, sorcery, sexual immorality, theft) recalls the Decalogue violations that brought covenant judgment on Israel; now the entire world stands in Israel's place, guilty of the same fundamental sins.

Osborne draws attention to the theological climax of the trumpet sequence in vv. 20-21. The entire judgment cycle—from the first trumpet's burning earth to the sixth trumpet's two-hundred-million cavalry—has had a single pastoral purpose: to provoke repentance. And it fails. This is the darkest revelation of the trumpet sequence: not the scale of destruction but the scale of human stubbornness. The human heart, confronted with overwhelming evidence of God's power and judgment, doubles down on its rebellion rather than submitting. This theological verdict sets up the need for a different approach—the prophetic witness of the church (chs. 10-11), which is the true instrument of God's redemptive work in the world.

hist.context: The sixth trumpet (second woe) releases four angels bound at the Euphrates River, who command a vast cavalry of two hundred million. The Euphrates was the eastern boundary of both the Promised Land (Gen 15:18) and the Roman Empire, and it carried deep symbolic freight: beyond the Euphrates lay Parthia, Rome's most feared enemy, whose mounted archers were the terror of the ancient world. The imagery fuses historical reality (Parthian cavalry) with supernatural horror (horses with lion heads, fire, smoke, and sulfur from their mouths, serpent-like tails). The result is a vision of combined human and demonic warfare that kills one-third of humanity (v. 18). Yet the most shocking revelation comes in the final verses (vv. 20-21): despite the deaths of one-third of the human race, 'the rest of mankind who were not killed by these plagues still did not repent.' This is the theological verdict on the trumpet sequence: escalating judgment fails to produce repentance. The human heart, apart from grace, is incurably resistant to God.

themes.note: Revelation 9 is the darkest chapter so far: judgment and sovereignty dominate as demonic forces torment and destroy under divine permission. Justice demands accountability for sin. Mercy is notably low—the chapter ends with the refusal of repentance, showing the limits of judgment without grace.

rows.label: vv.1-2
rows.text: Fallen star opens the Abyss; smoke darkens the sun

rows.label: vv.3-6
rows.text: Demonic locusts: restrictions and five months of torment

rows.label: vv.7-10
rows.text: Description of the locusts: composite nightmare

rows.label: v.12
rows.text: First woe past; two woes coming

rows.label: vv.13-15
rows.text: Sixth trumpet: four bound angels released at the Euphrates

rows.label: vv.16-19
rows.text: The cavalry of two hundred million

rows.label: vv.20-21
rows.text: Theological verdict: humanity refuses to repent

lit.note: Revelation 9 presents the two woe-trumpets in ascending severity: the fifth trumpet brings five months of demonic torment without death; the sixth trumpet brings the death of one-third of humanity. Both culminate in the refusal to repent (vv. 20-21), establishing the failure of judgment alone to accomplish God's redemptive purposes.

trans.title: Comparing translations in The Fifth and Sixth Trumpets

translations.text: When he opened the Abyss, smoke rose from it like the smoke from a gigantic furnace. The sun and sky were darkened by the smoke from the Abyss.
He opened the shaft of the bottomless pit, and from the shaft rose smoke like the smoke of a great furnace, and the sun and the air were darkened with the smoke from the shaft.
They were not allowed to kill them but only to torture them for five months. And the agony they suffered was like that of the sting of a scorpion when it strikes.
They were allowed to torment them for five months, but not to kill them, and their torment was like the torment of a scorpion when it stings someone.

Emphasises historical-critical, literary, or text-critical analysis.
Reads The Fifth and Sixth Trumpets within the canonical framework of fulfilled prophecy and covenant theology.
Represented by Calvin, MacArthur, and the evangelical tradition.
Both readings engage the text seriously; they differ primarily in their hermeneutical starting points and assumptions about authorship and historical context.

coaching.tip: Demonic locusts from the abyss (vv. 1-11) and a mounted army of 200 million (vv. 13-19) — imagery that defies literalism. Revelation uses symbols to convey realities beyond description. The point isn't identification of the army but recognition of judgment's horror. Imagination serves theology.

'The rest of mankind, who were not killed by these plagues, did not repent' (v. 20). Judgment alone doesn't produce repentance. Despite overwhelming evidence, hearts remain hard. This sober realism runs through Revelation: wrath can destroy but can't convert. Only the Lamb's blood changes hearts.
